---
applyTo: "lib/routes/world/**,lib/features/quests/repo/**,lib/features/activity_sessions/**"
description: "World map design — the discovery surface: what content appears, the personalized default, search/filters, and the small/mid/large pin tiers that preference open sessions."
---

# World Map

The map is the canvas the whole app sits on; every other surface is a panel over it (the workspace model and the `?c=` course-context param live in [routing.instructions.md](routing.instructions.md)). This doc covers what the map is _for_ and what lives _on_ it: the content, the personalized default, search, filters, and how items are displayed as pins. The base-tile and brand strategy is a separate concern — see [world-map-tiles.instructions.md](world-map-tiles.instructions.md). Google Maps is the guiding UI/UX reference.

## What the map is for

- **It is the place to discover essentially everything in Pangea Chat.** The job is to put the right thing in front of a learner at the right moment so they stay engaged and keep learning _socially_. Everything below serves that goal.
- **Preference open sessions.** Joining someone else's live activity session is more social and lower-friction than starting your own, so open, joinable sessions are always surfaced and always rank ahead of solo or not-yet-started content when the map decides what to feature. This recurs in ranking and display below because it is the map's strongest bias.
- **Never the whole library at once.** The map shows a curated, personalized, in-view subset, not a data dump; search and filters refine or widen from there. Legibility is a feature — a crowded map discovers nothing, which is why the display tiers below are an attention budget, not a rendering of everything available.
- **Show the learner's journey.** A path of little stars tracing a path around the world, representing the communicative goals achieved by the learner.

## What appears on the map

- **Today: located activities and their open sessions.** Geography lives on the activity (its coordinates), not on the course or quest — see the content model in [quests-and-learning-objectives](../../../.github/.github/instructions/quests-and-learning-objectives.instructions.md). The preferenced unit is an open **session**: a live, joinable instance of an activity.
- **Designed to generalize.** A map item is a located thing with facets, so new content types can join the same default → search → filter → display pipeline without a redesign: places and locations, opt-in users-as-content, and curated social items from the (future, supply-side) [world-feed](../../../.github/.github/instructions/world-feed.instructions.md).
- **A course scope narrows the candidate set.** The map scope is a [`MapContext`](../../lib/routes/world/map_context.dart) — [`WorldMapContext`](../../lib/routes/world/map_context.dart) for the whole world, [`CourseMapContext`](../../lib/routes/world/map_context.dart) for a single course. Under the `?c=` course context (see [routing.instructions.md](routing.instructions.md)) the map shows just that course's activities; the unscoped world view is the personalized default below. The content, ranking, and display pipeline is identical either way — the scope only changes which items compete.
- **The map changes focus only on a deliberate, focus-bearing selection.** A map focus is what the map is scoped or pointed at; it changes _only_ when you select something that carries one — a **course** (scopes to its activities) or an **activity** (camera glides to its pin) — or via the **World/home** control, the one focus that names no item and so opens to the broadest possible, the whole world. Tapping a surface that carries no map focus (the Courses hub, Chats, Settings) overlays the map you left without changing it (#7087). The scope/focus token model lives in [routing.instructions.md](routing.instructions.md).
- **The camera moves only for an activity focus or the explicit focus button — never for a course (#7616).** Focusing an activity **pans** its pin into the exposed canvas at the current zoom (no zoom change in either direction — the zoom-to-16 jump was disorienting). A course coming into context moves the camera **not at all**: with activities scattered across a course there is no single right place to auto-point at (the bounds fit zoomed jarringly; a pan-to-top-pin read as panning to nowhere), so the scope change speaks through the pins alone. The one camera path that zooms is the **focus button** on the activity plan page and the course card header: an activity glides in to `focusZoom` (never zooming out past the current view); a course zoom+pan-fits its activities' bounds.

## Discovering joinable sessions

A pin's `joinable` state (and the +3 that dominates ranking) is derived from the learner's own Matrix rooms **plus** two lightweight discovery reads, so a live session the learner has not personally joined still surfaces:

- **Invited sessions** — a session the learner was invited to is already in their room list, but the invite's stripped state does not carry `pangea.activity_roles` (only defaults + the room type), so accurate seats come from a `room_preview` read ([`getRoomSummaries`](../../lib/features/room_summaries/room_summary_extension.dart)), not local state.
- **Coursemate sessions in a joined course** — a session another member started is an `m.space.child` of the course space but is **not** in the learner's room list until they join it. The child state a coursemate wrote is not reliably in the learner's _local_ room state, so the client enumerates the course space's activity-session children from the **server-side** space hierarchy (`getSpaceHierarchy`, the same read the course page uses) and `room_preview`-s the ones the learner is not already in.

Both feed the same pure [signal reducer](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_signals.dart) as owned rooms, keyed by `activity_id`. A discovered session is surfaced as **joinable** while it is unfinished, **has a free seat**, and someone is **present** in it (at least one non-bot member currently joined) — the presence check keeps stale, abandoned rooms (started, then left, never finished) off the map, and the free-seat check keeps *full* sessions off it. **Both the map and the start page gate on the same `isActivityOpenToJoin` (`!isStarted`)**, so a pin the map shows joinable is one the start page will actually offer a Join for — never a green pin that dead-ends at "Start". Open-seat filtering works **where the preview carries the session's role plan** (`isStarted` = all roles taken); where the preview carries only a thin activity ref (no role plan) seats are unknown, `isStarted` is false, and the gate stays permissive (presence-only) exactly as before — so this refinement strictly tightens precision without regressing thin-ref sessions. This precision matters **more** under the heavy-tier gate ([Priority matrix](#priority-matrix)): there, a false-positive joinable (a session someone joined but abandoned without leaving) suppresses the prominence of _all_ other content, not merely adds a pin — so the gate raises the value of a future liveness/recency signal (part of the deferred cross-course discovery work, [Future Work](#future-work)). The discovery reads are throttled off the sync cadence. The **activity start page reuses this same discovery**: opening an activity the map already surfaced as joinable renders its "join open session" list from the cached preview data (a process-wide cache keyed by `activity_id`), so a pin tap needs no extra fetch; a deep-link open (cache miss) falls back to the start page's own read, with a loading indicator on the CTA meanwhile. Strangers' sessions **outside** the learner's joined courses stay out of reach until the choreographer exposes cross-course discovery ([Future Work](#future-work)).

## The personalized default

Before any search or filter, the map shows **my L2, at or below my CEFR, in the current viewport, colored by state with my progress shown** — derived from signals the app already holds about the learner, with no new data capture. This is not a separate code path: it is just the [filters](#filters) pre-seeded (L2, CEFR, joinable, joined quests), the _initial_ state and not a gate:

- Preferences joinable sessions across the learner's languages — their L1 and L2. (Support for multiple L1s and L2s is future work.)
- Changing or clearing those filters and searching refine it; a one-tap **reset** returns to it.
- When the view is empty, offer a **widen** affordance (all languages, or zoom out) so personalization never dead-ends.

## Pin display

Figma Frames: https://www.figma.com/design/n2qX4WsnVhYqT2KV6pMVbl/Everything-outside-of-Chat?node-id=13673-111364&t=0GRGq1KrTVyRx8Ho-0

### Small, mid, and large pins

The map holds far more than it should ever show at full weight, so every item renders at one of three visual weights ([`PinTier`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_ranking.dart)) and only a few earn the heavy ones. The counts are an **attention budget** driven by the **available visible-map width** (the viewport minus any open side panels). A single **breakpoint table** — in the dedicated tuning file [`world_map_pin_budget.dart`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_pin_budget.dart) — maps that width to a total cap **`N`** and its constituent **large / mid / small** caps, which **sum to `N`** (e.g. a mid width-band might be `N = 20 = large 3 + mid 6 + small 11`). As the width shrinks — panels open, or the bottom-nav/mobile layout — `N` falls and the **heavy tiers empty first**: at narrow widths there are no large cards, then no mid, leaving only small dots. Fewer is always better than crowding. The breakpoints and counts (and the tier pixel sizes) all live in that one file for easy tuning. The visual treatment is the [pin-tier frame](https://www.figma.com/design/n2qX4WsnVhYqT2KV6pMVbl/Everything-outside-of-Chat?node-id=12912-348899).

| Tier           | How many                                         | What it is                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Small dot**  | the `small` cap — the remainder of `N`           | a deliberately **small** plain dot — an available item, no detail until tapped, and **no activity-name label** (labels are a mid/large-only affordance, below). Dots render **individually** and may overlap; the map never collapses them into a count bubble (no clustering)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| **Mid pin**    | the `mid` cap (zero at narrow widths)            | a pin with a state icon **matching its large-card counterpart** — `joinable`: open-door icon + a "num/num" participant count; `ongoing`/pending: hourglass icon + "num/num"; `ongoing`/active: chat icon (no count) — plus an **activity-name label** (Google-Maps style): sits to the left or right of the pin, defaulting to the right but placed wherever the map has room. Label text is colored to match the pin's state color (green for `joinable`, dark purple for `ongoing`), except on `available` pins — whose fill is light purple/light brand, too low-contrast for light-purple text — where the label uses **dark purple** text instead; every label carries a thin **white outline/halo** for legibility over the map, same as Google Maps place labels. When a mid pin is promoted to a large card, its label moves inside the card as the title ([Large card](#small-mid-and-large-pins) above); when a live session is in view only `joined`/`joinable` sessions earn mid (the [Priority matrix](#priority-matrix) gate), else strong matches and open sessions earn it where the width allows |
| **Large card** | the `large` cap (zero below the width threshold) — and **always zero** when no `joinable`/`ongoing` session is in view, since `completed` and `available` are never eligible for this tier at any score | [`WorldMapLargeCard`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_large_card.dart) — a map **callout**, not a solid-color fill: a base-color background (white in light theme, near-black in dark) with a thick state-colored border and a pin triangle at the bottom pointing at the map location (mid and small pins are the inverse — solid state-colored fill with white icon/text — see [Pin state](#pin-state)). **Eligibility is a hard gate, not a score threshold**: only `joinable` and `ongoing` (`pending`/`active`) ever render as a large card; `completed` and `available` never do, and always fall back to a mid pin or small dot instead. Content differs by state: <br>• **Joinable** (green border) — the activity name centered on top, then below it a row with the open-door icon and the participant row (one avatar circle per role, filled if taken — the existing role-seat filled/unfilled visual). That's all: no image, type badge, level-match indicator, or star row. <br>• **Ongoing/pending** (dark-purple border) — the same layout as joinable, but an hourglass icon in place of the open door: the learner holds a role, but the room doesn't yet have enough people for the chat to have started. <br>• **Ongoing/active** (dark-purple border) — looks like a chat-list tile instead: the activity name on top in the same style as the other states, the last chat event beneath it, then the row of currently-gained stars at the bottom ([Goal Progress](#goal-progress)) — the **only** large-card state that shows stars. <br>Tapping the card opens the activity's detail panel directly, like any pin — there is no tap-to-peek ([Interaction](#interaction)). When no `joinable`/`ongoing` session is in view, the large tier renders **nothing** — there is no next-Mission or available-activity fallback; that slot in the width budget simply goes unused ([Priority matrix](#priority-matrix)). |

A pin's **state** ([`ActivityPinState`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_ranking.dart)) is carried by **color** at every tier ([Pin state](#pin-state) below). Every activity is always playable, so progression only ever _ranks_ content, never gates it (the [Priority matrix](#priority-matrix) and [quests.instructions.md](quests.instructions.md)).

**No clustering — small pins render individually (Google-Maps style).** The map does not group long-tail pins into a count bubble. Every pin the [Priority matrix](#priority-matrix) admits (the top-`N` for the current width) renders on its own; a small pin is a deliberately **small dot**, and dots are allowed to overlap rather than collapsing into a bubble.

Two large cards never claim overlapping screen space (the lower-scored yields to a mid pin or dot), and a large card with no room at a screen edge **is downgraded to a mid or small pin** rather than spilling off. (Focusing an activity centers the camera on it, so a focused card lands in view rather than at an edge; the camera **edge-nudge** built for the old tap-to-peek is kept but rarely needed now that one tap goes straight to focus.) The placement mechanism is step 4 of the [Priority matrix](#priority-matrix) pipeline.

Small pins should never show on top of mid or large pins. Mid pins should never show on top of large pins.

### Pin state

A pin's **state** ([`ActivityPinState`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_ranking.dart)) is carried by color AND shape for accessibility — the `Activity pin v3` component in the Figma frame linked above.

| State           | Look                                    | Meaning                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| --------------- | --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Ongoing**      | Dark brand purple. On mid/small pins: solid dark-purple fill, and (mid only) a dark-purple activity-name label with a white outline. Icon depends on sub-state: **Pending** — hourglass icon + "num/num" participant count. **Active** — chat icon (no count). As a **large card**: base (white/black) background, thick dark-purple border, pin triangle — never a solid purple fill on a large card. **Pending** — activity name on top, then a row with an hourglass icon and the participant row (filled/unfilled avatar circles, one per role). **Active** — looks like a chat-list tile: activity name on top in the same style, the last chat event beneath it, then the row of currently-gained stars at the bottom. | the learner holds a role in a session they have **not finished**. **Pending** = waiting for other participants (the chat hasn't started); **Active** = the session/chat has started |
| **Joinable**    | Green. On mid/small pins: solid green fill with a white door-open icon and a "num/num" format showing current participants, and (mid only) a green activity-name label with a white outline. As a **large card**: base (white/black) background, thick green border, pin triangle — the activity name centered on top, then a row with the door-open icon and the participant row (filled/unfilled avatar circles, one per role). No image, type, level match, or star row. | an open session is live that the learner can **join** (someone else's) — the map's preferred unit                                                                                                |
| **Completed** | small dot on the map, never a pin or card, and never an activity-name label (dots never carry one). Can have another pin of type ongoing or joinable stacked on top of it. It's either a **star** which is a single gold star in a base color circle, or a **super star** which is a gold star_shine icon in a base color circle.                              | A **star** means that the user has completed all goals of one role in this activity in at least one previous attempt. A **super star** means that through all their attempts, they have completed every star possible in this activity. |
| **Available**   | light brand/light purple, a plus icon in the middle of the pin. No large card version. On mid pins, still carries an activity-name label like the other states, but in **dark-purple** text rather than the pin's own light-purple (too low-contrast) — same white outline as the rest. Renders at **50% opacity** (pin, and label on mid pins) when the course can't currently staff the activity — see [Understaffed pins](#understaffed-pins). Does **not** appear on previously completed activities that have either type of star on the map. | playable, nothing live and no stars yet — the default                                                                                                                                            |

An activity plan can have completed sessions, joinable sessions and ongoing as well as being generally available. To resolve into one display, we choose state as the first that matches the conditions for **Ongoing** → **Joinable** → **Available**. A completed star dot can be showed on the map under an ongoing, or joinable pin. It replaces available pins. A live session always wins state over joinable, joinable always wins over available, and completed always wins over the plain default. 

A **pinged** modifier (a hand glyph in a dark yellow circle) marks an open session whose host has pinged the course to gather players (mechanics in [activities.instructions.md](activities.instructions.md)). A ping leaves no persistent room state, so **pinged** is detected **best-effort** by scanning recent course-space messages for the host's ping — a proxy whose efficacy we watch before investing in a persistent signal. The ping is an `m.text` posted to the course **space**, so it also bumps that space's unread badge; the world UI has no course-space timeline to open and read, so the map **clears that badge once it has consumed the ping** — the pinned pin (which shows *which* activity) is the real surface, and course spaces carry nothing but structural events and pings, so clearing never hides real content (#7366). The scan runs on the map's initial load and on sync, so pings surface — and their badge clears — within the session, not only across a reload. Which items earn the scarce mid and large slots is the [Priority matrix](#priority-matrix) below.

### Understaffed pins

An `available` pin can point at an activity the course **cannot currently fill**: the bot fills exactly one role, so an activity needs `roleCount − 1` human members, and a small course may not have that many yet (2 members can't fill a 4-role activity even with the bot invited — that gets them to 3; a solo member can only fill a 2-role activity). When an `available` pin's activity is understaffed this way, the pin renders at **50% opacity** — the pin itself, plus the activity-name label on a mid pin (a small dot has no label to dim). This is purely cosmetic: the pin still taps, focuses, and opens the activity's plan page exactly as normal. Only `available` pins dim — `joinable`/`ongoing` pins are themselves proof enough humans are already present, and a `completed` star dot replaces the available pin outright (per the state precedence above) regardless of current headcount. This is distinct from, and outlasts, the `multi_person_first_map` score term ([Priority matrix](#priority-matrix)): that term only demotes ranking for a learner's very first pick and clears after their first activity, whereas a small course can stay genuinely short-handed indefinitely, and the dimming keeps signaling that for as long as it's true.

### Goal Progress

A learner's progress in an activity is the stars earned toward its total (a star is one awarded activity goal — [activities.instructions.md](activities.instructions.md)); earned stars are read from Matrix room state by [`QuestsClientExtension`](../../lib/features/quests/quests_client_extension.dart), matched on the awarded goal's `goal_slug`. The **total** (the denominator) is the activity's goal count for the learner's role, which comes from the pin's thin **`goals`** (`role_ids` picks the learner's role, `goal_slug`/`id` keys completion) — so the star row renders on **any** pin without hydrating the full plan; a full-plan hydration (large-card open) refines it but is not required. Progress shows two ways, by tier:

- **Progress is only showed on the dots/pins if a role has been completed (every star has been gained in at least one role)** Partial progress is not shown in available, joinable, or completed states. Completed dots will either show a star (one role completed) or a super star (all roles completed). The star/super star dot is always shown on the map, even if the activity is not joinable or ongoing. If an activity is finished, but all roles are still incomplete, the activity will show as available (unless there are joinable or ongoing instances)
- **The only large card that shows stars is an ongoing active card**, the star row is displayed at the bottom of the card beneath the title and last event.

### Tap any pin to Focus

**Tapping any pin focuses the activity**: Tapping a dot zooms in until the dot can be promoted to a mid pin, and focuses this pin. Tapping a pin centers/focuses that pin in the camera view, and opens the activity's **plan page** (a left panel beside the map on a wide screen; on narrow a half-open bottom sheet with the pin still visible above — the Google Maps UX). If the focused pin is `joinable`/`ongoing` (large-eligible), the same tap also raises it to a **large card** — even if the `large` cap was already full elsewhere, evicting the current lowest-scoring large card down a tier ([Priority matrix step 3](#priority-matrix)); an `available`/`completed` pin still focuses and opens the plan page, it just never becomes a large card, however it's tapped. There is **no hover/preview popup** between those states on any width. Pin tiers and large-card placement don't change while the camera is actively panning or zooming — they hold whatever was last placed, and update together once the camera settles (the debounced re-rank in [Priority matrix step 4](#priority-matrix) below).

It carries a distinct **focused marker** at whatever size it currently sits — the "which one is open" indicator, and the only one in the dots-only state.

### Dismissing a large card (the X)

Every large card carries an **X** whose action is **demote, never remove** (#7207): the activity leaves the large tier and re-renders at whatever lighter tier it earns — a mid pin where eligible, else a dot — so dismissing never hides content, it only reclaims screen space (its vacated large slot back-fills from the next-highest-scoring **eligible** — `joinable`/`ongoing` — candidate, if any; otherwise, per the [Priority matrix](#priority-matrix) gate, the slot just stays empty). A dismissal **lapses after 10 minutes** (`WorldMapDismissals.ttl`), so an X is never a permanent burial — an activity that later earns real attention (say a joinable session opens on it) returns to large contention within the sitting; expiry re-ranks even an idle map (a timer, not just the next pan/sync). While it runs, a dismissal acts through **both** halves of the ranking system: a **−0.5 `dismissed` score term** (the [Priority matrix](#priority-matrix) — same magnitude and meaning as `completed`: demote, keep on the map) *and* **large-tier ineligibility** in the placement pass. The weight alone can't make the X reliable — a dismissed pin with no competition (the sole live session in view) would stay top-ranked and instantly re-take the card — and an eligibility rule alone wouldn't sink it within the mid/small ordering; together the X always visibly works and stays inside the one scoring system. Without any memory at all the next re-rank would immediately re-promote the card, making the X a no-op. On a **focused** card the X also **clears focus**, identically to closing the detail panel, so an open panel never points at a non-large pin. A dismissed activity can still be focused again by tapping its dot/mid pin — the panel opens as usual, but the pin stays at its lighter tier (an exception to tap-promotes-to-card above: the learner said "no card for this one", and the focus ring plus panel already carry the context).

Two axes, kept separate: the **width-driven caps** (the breakpoint table's `N` and its large/mid/small split — [Priority matrix](#priority-matrix)) decide _how many_ of each tier show and the **relevance score** decides _which_ pins fill them, while **focus** is the lone deliberate user act on top. There is deliberately **no separate "selected"/peek state** — a tap on an eligible pin promotes it straight to a focused large card (evicting the weakest current one if the cap was full) — so the same mechanism (ranking + width caps + focus) covers a wide desktop and a panel-crowded or mobile layout alike.

## Priority matrix

Every item is ranked by **one weighted score**, and nothing is ever locked from being *shown*. The **large tier is the one hard eligibility gate**: only `joinable` and `ongoing` (a live session) can ever render as a large card — `completed` and `available` never do, at any score or width ([Pin display](#pin-display)). Below that gate, a **live session** (joinable or joined) is not a separate stage but the heaviest term, so mid/small ordering — and which live session wins the large slot when several qualify — still collapses into a single number. Starting score:

`score = 3·joined + 2·joinable + relevance_band + 0.6·pinged + 0.3·recency − 0.5·completed − 0.5·dismissed − 2·multi_person_first_map`

Signal inputs are [`PinSignals`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_ranking.dart), derived by [`WorldMapSignalUtils`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_signals.dart); the ranked outcome per item is [`RankingResult`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_ranking.dart).

- **joined** `0/1` — a live session the learner is **already in** (holds a role). The single most important thing is engaging in your active chats, and finishing them.`joinable` and `joined` are **mutually exclusive** per pin (the [state](#pin-state) precedence picks one), so at most one of the two ever fires: a live session scores **+3 if are already in it, +2 if you can join it**. Both are tunable levers (below).
- **joinable** `0/1` — an **open** live session the learner can join right now (someone else's). Weighted second heaviest because joining someone's live session is a major point of the map (see [What the map is for](#what-the-map-is-for)). The large tier itself is gated to `joinable`/`ongoing` (above), not scored into eligibility — when nothing is live, the large tier is simply empty rather than falling back to a next-Mission item. This weight instead decides which live session wins the slot when several qualify (`joined` outranks `joinable`), and orders `joinable` among everything else within mid/small.
- **relevance_band** `0–2` — the next-Mission gradient, below.
- **pinged** `0/1` — the open session's host is recruiting ([activities.instructions.md](activities.instructions.md)).
- **recency** `0–1` — newest first, a linear falloff over the last day (for a session, time since it opened).
- **completed** `0/1` — the learner has a full star row on _this activity_; it demotes so a done activity stays on the map (the trail) without taking a slot meant for the next thing to do. This is activity-level completion, distinct from Mission satisfaction in the band.
- **dismissed** `0/1` — the learner X'd this activity's large card within the last 10 minutes ([Dismissing](#dismissing-a-large-card-the-x)); the same demote-keep-visible shape as `completed`. The term sinks a dismissed pin within the mid/small ordering; the guarantee that it never renders large while dismissed is the placement-pass eligibility rule, not this weight.
- **multi_person_first_map** `0/1` — the learner has **no prior activity yet** (this is their first pick from the map) _and_ the activity needs **3+ roles** _and_ it is not itself a live session. The bot fills exactly one role, so a 3+ role activity cannot be started solo (it needs a second human); on a friendless new learner's first map that is a confusing dead-end, so it is pushed down hard — a **large but finite** weight: deprioritized toward the small-dot tail, **not gated** (#7435). It clears the moment the learner **starts, joins, or finishes any activity** (`Client.hasAnyActivitySession`). Two-role activities are unaffected — the bot fills the second seat, so they are solo-viable and make good first/tutorial activities. Role count comes from the pin's **`roleCount`** (the thin `roles` list on the bbox card). A live **`joinable`/'ongoing'** 3+ session is never penalized — other humans are already present to fill the seats — though a brand-new learner rarely has one in view. This score term is separate from, and clears sooner than, the [Understaffed pins](#understaffed-pins) opacity treatment: this one only demotes a first-timer's ranking and clears after their first activity, that one dims `available` pins by course headcount for as long as the course is actually short-handed.

**Relevance is a next-Mission gradient that accumulates across quests.** For each quest in scope (the learner's joined courses by default, or whatever the quest filter selects), the client finds the quest's **anchor Mission** — the Mission the learner most needs next — and scores activities by how close their Missions sit to it. The anchor is the **first Mission in quest order whose star total is below the satisfaction threshold**; once every Mission is satisfied, the anchor becomes the **lowest-star Mission**, so a completed quest keeps pointing the learner at their weakest area instead of going flat. An activity's contribution _from one quest_ rises to the top of the band when it carries that anchor and **decays smoothly** for Missions further along; a Mission that is **already satisfied contributes essentially nothing**, so the band never preferences work the learner has finished. When several quests are in scope, an activity's contributions **sum across them (saturating at the band ceiling)** rather than taking the single best — so an activity that advances _several_ quests' still-unsatisfied Missions at once outranks one that advances only a single quest. That falls out of the same per-Mission weighting; there is no separate multi-quest branch, and the saturation keeps the band under the heavier `joinable` term. Outside any quest there is no anchor, so an activity scores on plain level/L2 fit — the same ranking, just without a frontier to pull toward. The anchors and star rollup are resolved by the shared client resolver in [quests.instructions.md](quests.instructions.md).

**The data the band reads** lives on the pin and in client-resolved progression. Each bbox card from `/v2/activities/bbox` ([`ActivityMapRepo`](../../lib/features/quests/repo/activity_map_repo.dart)) carries the activity's **level** (CEFR), **L2**, and **Mission refs** (`learning_objective_refs`, parsed into [`QuestActivityCard`](../../lib/features/quests/models/quest_activity_card.dart)), which [`relevanceBand`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_ranking.dart) reads directly off the pin. The learner's in-scope quests and per-Mission star rollup come from [`QuestProgressionResolver`](../../lib/features/quests/quest_progression_resolver.dart), cached in [`JoinedObjectiveCache`](../../lib/routes/world/joined_objective_cache.dart) and refreshed on course join/leave rather than per re-rank. The band itself needs no new CMS field. The bbox card **also** carries cheap **non-localized** structural metadata the pin uses beyond banding — the thin **`roles`** (→ `roleCount`, for the `multi_person_first_map` term above), thin **`goals`** (`{id, goal_slug, role_ids, phase}`, for star progress **without a full-plan hydration** — see [Goal Progress](#goal-progress)), plus **`mode`** and the rating — with the localized copy (role personas, goal text, vocab, media) dropped so pages stay cheap. The one always-cheap addition to the `/v2/activities/bbox` card contract; the count is free server-side (the plan is already in memory).

**The bbox is the one always-on filter; the width-driven caps are assigned over its survivors.** The current map viewport is a constant member of the [filter set](#filters) — never removable, continuously changing as the learner pans and zooms. The **available visible-map width** (the viewport minus any open side panels) selects a row of the breakpoint table ([`world_map_pin_budget.dart`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_pin_budget.dart)): a total cap **`N`** and its constituent **large / mid / small** caps that sum to `N`. Filling is emergent with **no change to any item's score** — walking the score-ranked survivors top-down, each earns the heaviest tier whose cap isn't yet full (large → mid → small); once `N` are placed, the rest are **not drawn** (see [Scale boundary](#scale-boundary)). Zoom changes _which_ candidates are in the bbox, not the caps, so a given activity is a small dot among many when zoomed far out and earns a heavier tier as the field thins on zoom-in. As the **width** shrinks (panels open, bottom-nav/mobile) the caps drop and the heavy tiers empty first, so opening panels — not only zooming — collapses the map toward dots; a focused activity whose own panel narrows the map past the threshold becomes a dot like the rest. The large cap is **further bounded by what physically fits** (step 4), so it is a ceiling, not a guarantee.

**The pipeline.** Like a feed, the map retrieves a candidate set, scores it, then re-ranks for diversity:

1. **Candidates** — the filtered set inside the current bbox.
2. **Score** — the single formula above.
3. **Fill the caps, with diversity — `large` is always live-only; `mid` is conditionally live-only.** The width breakpoint gives the `large`, `mid`, and `small` caps (summing to `N`). **`large` is categorically restricted to `ongoing`/`joinable` — always, not only when one happens to be in view: `completed` and `available` are never eligible for `large`, at any score** ([Pin display](#pin-display)). Separately, **when the current view holds _any_ `ongoing` or `joinable` session, `mid` is also restricted to those** — every other pin (`available`) renders `small`, however high it scores; **when nothing live is in view, that `mid`-tier gate is inert** and `mid` fills by score as before (the next-Mission frontier activity can still earn a **mid** pin this way — it can never earn `large`). This is a deliberate escalation of the map's core preference-open-sessions bias from a soft score weight to a hard tier gate: with a live session in view, the map makes _what to join_ prominent and lets everything else recede to dots; and even with nothing live in view, `large` stays reserved for a future live session rather than ever being spent on non-live content. Then fill as usual: walk the score-ordered **eligible** list top-down into the heaviest tier whose cap isn't yet full (large → mid → small), avoiding a heavy-tier set that is all one course or Mission, and place the rest as `small` up to `N`. `N` (the on-screen count) is unchanged — ineligible pins drop a tier, they don't vanish. If the view holds **fewer** live sessions than the `large` (+ `mid`, when gated) caps, the surplus heavy capacity simply goes **unused** (fewer large/mid pins) — it is _never_ back-filled with non-live content, and with **zero** live sessions in view, `large` renders nothing at all. Reserve up to the per-width `trail` count of the `N` slots for the highest-ranked in-view **progressed** activities so the learner's trail is not crowded out ([Goal Progress](#goal-progress)) — these always render `small` (gold-star dots), per [Pin state](#pin-state) (`completed` is never a pin or card). When more live sessions tie for `large` than its cap (the common case: several joinable at once), the large slots **rotate through them, about every 5 seconds**; with a cap of three or fewer they stay static. When the `large`/`mid` caps are zero (narrow width), all `N` render as small dots and both gates are moot. **Focus overrides score for its own slot**: if the learner taps and focuses an eligible (`joinable`/`ongoing`) pin that the score-only pass would have left at `mid` because the `large` cap was already full, it still takes a large slot — evicting the lowest-scoring current large occupant down a tier (mid, if it's still itself eligible, else small) — so tapping an eligible pin always raises it to large, never strands it at mid ([Tap any pin to Focus](#tap-any-pin-to-focus)).
4. **Place** — Large cards no longer collapse to their lighter tiers for the duration of a zoom gesture (the old #7245 behavior): whatever was placed before the gesture started simply holds until the next settle re-rank (the pan/zoom debounce described below) recomputes candidates, scores, and placement fresh at the new camera. Zoom now behaves exactly like pan always has — nothing about pin display changes until the camera settles. Then: a large card is a _box_, not a point, so the large slice is laid onto real screen positions last (mid and small ride the plain dot layer; this step is large-only, and is **skipped entirely when the large cap is zero, or when no `joinable`/`ongoing` candidates are in view**). Walking the large candidates top-down, a card earns its slot only if its footprint fits the **unclaimed visible safe area**: on-screen — not off an edge, not under an open panel (the `leftOverlayWidth` / `rightOverlayWidth` tracked by [`WorldMapController`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map.dart)) — and not overlapping a card already placed this pass. Footprints are reserved **focused-first, then by score**, so a focused card keeps its space and the rest of the large set yields around it. A candidate that cannot fit yields its slot to the next one down-score that can, and renders as its dot; the large count is therefore **emergent — `min(large cap, what fits)`**. Dots whose point falls under a placed card simply overlap it (no bubble forms). Focusing already centers the camera on the activity, so a focused card rarely lands at an edge; the legacy edge-nudge is retained but seldom triggers.

Re-ranking is debounced on pan and zoom (as the working-set re-fetch already is), and tier assignment is kept stable enough between nearby frames that a small pan does not reshuffle the cards. This runs client-side over the loaded set even while server-side viewport narrowing is deferred (see [Scale boundary](#scale-boundary)).

**Weights are levers, learned later.** The weights are hand-set starting points, tuned by observation: at our scale there isn't the engagement data to learn them from, and hand-set weights stay predictable and editable here — raising `joinable` / `ongoing` or `pinged` pushes the map harder toward live, social practice (and the `joinable` − `ongoing` gap sets how much the map favours joining _others_ over resuming your own session); deepening the band's decay sharpens the pull toward the next Mission. A learned value-model — predicting join / complete / return — is the upgrade once the data exists. **Interests** become a term once captured (not tracked today).

## Search

[`WorldMapSearchOverlay`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_search_overlay.dart) — search matches content in the relevant view — title, description, and learning objective — results-as-you-type; selecting a result flies the camera to it and opens its preview. Matching runs over the card's CANONICAL text: thin cards are never localized (choreo #2736 — description and learning objective ride on the card purely to enrich this matching, and are never rendered on a pin), so a learner may match against source-language copy; localized text appears when a result or pin is opened. Searching what is already loaded in view is the starting scope; off-view / global search is a later option.

## Filters

[`WorldMapFilter`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_filter.dart) is the single mechanism that defines the candidate set; the [Priority matrix](#priority-matrix) then ranks within it. The set is:

- **Viewport (lat/long bbox)** — the one **constant** filter: always applied, never removable, driven by the map camera. Its role in tiering is in the Priority matrix above.
- **Selected quests**, **target language (L2)**, **CEFR level**, and **a set of Missions** — optional, all-via-a-dropdown. Selecting a quest also sets the next-Mission anchor that the band ranks toward.
- **Free text** — typed in the search bar ([Search](#search)).

The optional filters are seeded from the personalized default (my L2, my CEFR, my joined quests) and a one-tap **reset** restores them; each active one shows below the search bar as an **X-able pill**. This replaces the always-on toggles that previously sat under the bar. **Completion state, theme, and interests** come later, once the content model carries them and a captured-interests signal exists; interests are a deliberate captured preference, never proxied from free text.

Design intent: a change to _which_ items exist widens or narrows the candidate set (a re-query); a change that only hides already-loaded items refines in place. Keep the cheap, indexed dimensions as set-changers and the rest as in-view refinements so the map stays responsive.

## Scale boundary

Map content is fetched through a **stable server contract** (the choreographer), not direct client-to-CMS queries, so the display and interaction design here is insulated from how the server narrows results. The catalog is currently small enough to return all placed items and apply the CEFR band on the client; true viewport-narrowing and server-side CEFR banding are deferred until density warrants them. Two caps stack: the server's **working-set fetch cap** (when more items match than it returns, the map **signals that more exist** rather than over-fetching), and the client's **render cap `N`** — the top-`N` survivors by score that actually draw, set by the available visible _width_ via the breakpoint table ([Priority matrix](#priority-matrix); [`world_map_pin_budget.dart`](../../lib/routes/world/world_map_pin_budget.dart)). The render cap is what keeps the map Google-Maps-sparse: zooming and filtering narrow the in-view candidate set while the cap holds the on-screen count, with **no clustering layer** in between (past `N`, items are simply not drawn — never collapsed into a count bubble). Track the density triggers in [scaling-watchlist](../../../.github/.github/instructions/scaling-watchlist.instructions.md).

## Future Work

File GitHub issues for these and link them here (use the `update-future-work` skill). Deferred design threads: a backend endpoint for **cross-course open-session discovery** — the client now surfaces joinable sessions in the learner's **joined** courses and ones they've been **invited** to (see [Discovering joinable sessions](#discovering-joinable-sessions)), but strangers' sessions in courses the learner has _not_ joined still need the choreographer to expose them (the core preference-open-sessions goal at full map-wide reach); a **session liveness/recency signal** (last-activity time) so the map can tell a genuinely-active open session from one a member joined but abandoned — the gap the heavy-tier gate makes more visible; evaluating the **best-effort pinged detection** (recent course-space message scan) before adding a persistent ping signal; a generalized map-item pipeline for non-activity content; users-as-content (opt-in location); world-feed social items as a content source; server-side viewport narrowing and CEFR banding.
